perturbation.original_text: "Conf idential Information " means any information disclosed by either party to the other party, either directly or indirectly, in writing, orally or by inspection of tangible objects, including without limitation documents, prototypes, samples, plant and equipme nt, research, product plans, products, services, customer lists, software, developments, inventions, processes, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration, marketing materials or finances, which is designated as "Confidential," "Proprietary" or some similar designation.
perturbation.changed_text: "Conf idential Information " means any information disclosed by either party to the other party, either directly or indirectly, in writing, orally or by inspection of tangible objects, including without limitation documents, prototypes, samples, plant and equipme nt, research, product plans, products, services, customer lists, software, developments, inventions, processes, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration, marketing materials or finances, whether or not it is designated as "Confidential," "Proprietary" or some similar designation.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for designation of information as 'Confidential' broadens the definition beyond what is typically protected under trade secret laws, potentially encompassing publicly available or non-proprietary information. Trade secrets must be actively protected to maintain their status.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes Section 688.002 defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable, and is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. By removing the requirement that information be designated as confidential, the agreement attempts to protect information regardless of whether reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy, contradicting the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for information to be designated as 'Confidential' or 'Proprietary' to be considered confidential. This contradicts Florida Statute 688.002(4)(b), which states that a 'trade secret' is information that 'is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' By attempting to protect information regardless of efforts to maintain secrecy, the modified agreement undermines the statutory definition of a trade secret, making the contract unenforceable to the degree it attempts to protect information beyond actual trade secrets. Therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Term . The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall surviv e for a period of five years after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as all Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.changed_text: Term . The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall surviv e indefinitely after the disclosure of the Confidential Information, regardless of whether Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the term to 'indefinitely' and removing the exception for publicly known information contradicts trade secret law, which protects information only as long as it remains secret and provides an unfair competitive advantage. Publicly available information cannot be a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes Section 688.002 defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. By stating the obligations survive 'indefinitely', it ignores the provision that information has to be 'not generally known', therefore contradicting Florida's trade secret law.

F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by extending the confidentiality obligations 'indefinitely' and removing the exception for publicly known information. This contradicts Florida Statute 688.002(4)(a), which defines a trade secret as information that 'derives independent economic value from not being generally known'. By not accounting for information becoming public, the changed text attempts to protect information that no longer qualifies as a trade secret under Florida law, making the clause unenforceable to that extent.

perturbation.original_text: Return of Materials . All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and re main the property of the disclosing party and shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party upon the disclosing party's written request.
perturbation.changed_text: Return of Materials . All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Information, whether confidential or not, which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and re main the property of the disclosing party and shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party upon the disclosing party's written request.
perturbation.explanation: Broadening the scope to include 'information, whether confidential or not' attempts to claim ownership and require return of information that lacks the characteristics of a trade secret (e.g., publicly available information). This exceeds the bounds of trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes Section 688.002 defines a trade secret. Requiring that materials containing non-confidential information be returned misrepresents and overextends the definition of what is protectable under the Florida Trade Secret Act.

F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the scope of 'Return of Materials' to include 'Information, whether confidential or not.' This contradicts Florida Statute 688.002, which defines 'trade secret' as information deriving economic value from not being generally known and subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By requiring the return of all information, regardless of confidentiality or trade secret status, the contract attempts to protect information beyond the statutory definition, thereby misaligning with established legal terminology. The statute explicitly defines 'trade secret' based on specific criteria, and the contract's overbroad definition undermines this statutory limitation.

perturbation.original_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED "AS IS." NEIT HER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES, EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR OTHERWISE, REGARDING ITS ACCURACY, COMPLETENESS OR PERFORMANCE.
perturbation.changed_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED "AS IS." NEIT HER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES, EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR OTHERWISE, REGARDING ITS ACCURACY OR COMPLETENESS. The disclosing party warrants the Confidential Information is free from any defects that would cause harm to the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a warranty regarding the absence of defects directly contradicts the 'AS IS' provision and creates a contradiction. Providing the information 'AS IS' means there are no warranties for anything, including safety. This can be problematic if the confidential information leads to direct damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: While the NDA is not a sales contract, warranting that the information is free from defects introduces an element similar to the implied warranty of merchantability under the UCC, which is usually disclaimed by an 'AS IS' clause. Introducing a warranty contradicts the intent of the 'AS IS' clause, creating ambiguity about whether the receiving party can claim damages based on any defects in the Confidential Information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a warranty that the confidential information is free from defects, which directly contradicts the 'AS IS' provision in the original contract. The 'AS IS' clause, by its nature, disclaims all warranties, including any warranty about the absence of defects or safety. The addition of a warranty undermines the purpose of the 'AS IS' clause, creating a legal contradiction. Although the snippets discuss 'implied warranty of merchantability' under the UCC, and the NDA is not a sales contract, the Law Explanation notes that introducing a warranty contradicts the intent of the 'AS IS' clause, and creates ambiguity. This is supported by the perturbation explanation. The added warranty creates an obligation that did not exist in the original contract, weakening the 'AS IS' provision. The 'AS IS' clause means the information comes without any guarantee; adding 'free from any defects' creates a guarantee and thus, a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Florida, U.S.A., as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state.
perturbation.changed_text: Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, U.S.A., as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Florida to Delaware without any connection to the state of Delaware could be considered forum shopping and potentially unenforceable, especially if it impacts the application of relevant statutes such as the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 688.003 pertains to injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. If the contract changes the governing law to Delaware, but the actual trade secret misappropriation occurred and caused damages within Florida, the choice of law provision might conflict with Florida's interest in protecting its businesses and trade secrets, potentially affecting the availability and scope of remedies under Florida law.
perturbation.location: Section titled 'Choice of Laws and Forum'

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Florida to Delaware introduces a contradiction because it potentially undermines the application of Florida Statute § 688.003, which provides injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets within Florida. The scraped law snippet explicitly mentions "Injunctive relief" under Florida Statutes Title XXXIX, Chapter 688, Section 003, indicating Florida's specific legal framework for trade secret protection. By changing the governing law, the contract attempts to circumvent Florida's interest in protecting businesses operating within its jurisdiction and the remedies available under Florida law for trade secret misappropriation, especially if the misappropriation occurred in Florida.
